original_abstract:
In this paper, we investigate the joint optimization of radio and computational resources within a MIMO multicell Mobile-Edge Computing (MEC) system. We address the challenge of migrating computationally intensive tasks from energy-constrained mobile devices to more powerful cloud servers located at the edge of the network. The problem is formulated as the minimization of the total energy consumption across all mobile users, subject to strict latency and transmit power constraints. This involves a simultaneous search for the optimal transmit precoding matrices for the users and the optimal CPU cycle allocation provided by the cloud.

The resulting optimization problem is non-convex in both the objective function and the constraints, posing significant analytical challenges. However, for the single-user case, we demonstrate that the problem can be transformed and solved to global optimality in closed form. For the more complex multiuser scenario, we develop an iterative algorithmic framework based on a novel successive convex approximation (SCA) technique. We prove that this algorithm converges to a local optimal solution of the original non-convex problem. Furthermore, we show that our framework is inherently suited for distributed and parallel implementation across radio access points, requiring minimal signaling and coordination with the central cloud. Numerical evaluations confirm that our joint optimization approach significantly outperforms traditional disjoint resource allocation schemes, offering substantial energy savings for next-generation mobile applications.
humanized_abstract:
In this paper, the joint optimization of the radio and computational resources in a MIMO multicell MEC system is investigated. In today’s mobile networks, resource demanding applications are processed at the mobile devices. This results in huge energy consumption at mobile devices. The main goal of MEC is to offload the tasks from resource constrained mobile devices to the cloud servers located at the edge of the network. Therefore, the total energy consumption of all the mobile users is minimized under the constraints of latency and transmit power. This means finding the optimal precoding matrices for the users and the optimal CPU cycle provided by the cloud.

The resulting optimization problem is non-convex in both the objective and the constraints. Although this fact makes the problem very difficult to analyze, we are able to transform the single-user problem and obtain an exact global solution in closed form. Then, the multiuser case is tackled by means of an iterative algorithm based on the use of Successive Convex Approximation (SCA) methodology. The optimality of the algorithm is proved, in the sense that it converges to a local optimum of the non-convex problem. In addition, this optimization framework is highly adequate for distributed/parallel implementation over the distributed Radio Access Points (RAPs), as it only requires low amount of centralized cloud exchanges. The performance benefits of the proposed joint optimization resource allocation framework is validated by means of extensive numerical evaluations, where the proposed approach is shown to provide one order of magnitude better energy efficiency than disjoint resource allocation approaches, which is highly desirable for emerging energy-aware mobile applications.

document.output:

document.input:
In this paper, we investigate the joint optimization of radio and computational resources within a MIMO multicell Mobile-Edge Computing (MEC) system. We address the challenge of migrating computationally intensive tasks from energy-constrained mobile devices to more powerful cloud servers located at the edge of the network. Numerical evaluations confirm that our joint optimization approach significantly outperforms traditional disjoint resource allocation schemes, offering substantial energy savings for next-generation mobile applications.
